title: W1 C Euclidean Spaces
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 1C    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/10
 Euclidean Spaces




                   2/10
                    Numbers and More Numbers

◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

◦ The set of all rational numbers is denoted Q:

                      Numbers and More Numbers

  ◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

                                                   

Observation N is countably infinite.




                                                                           3/10
                      Numbers and More Numbers


  ◦ The set of all natural numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, . . . is denoted N.

  ◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

  ◦ The set of all rational numbers is denoted Q:
                                                   
                                p
                         Q =      : p, q ∈ Z, q ̸= 0 .
                                q

Observation N is countably infinite.

Z and Q have the same cardinality as N —they are also countably infinite.
  ◦ There is a one-to-one association between N and each of these sets.

  ◦ Let’s look at Hilbert’s Hotel.




                                                                            3/10
                    Numbers and More Numbers


To show that Z is countably infinite:
  ◦ Set z = n2 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is even.

  ◦ Set z = − n−1
               2
                  ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is odd.




                                                     4/10
                     Numbers and More Numbers


To show that Z is countably infinite:
  ◦ Set z = n2 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is even.

  ◦ Set z = − n−1
               2
                  ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is odd.

Showing that Q is countably infinite turns out to be equivalent to showing
that the set of ordered pairs N × N is countably infinite.

                 (1, 1)    (1, 2)       (1, 3)   (1, 4)      ...
                 (2, 1)    (2, 2)       (2, 3)   (2, 4)      ···
                 (3, 1)    (3, 2)       (3, 3)   (3, 4)      ···
                 (4, 1)    (4, 2)       (4, 3)   (4, 4)      ···
                   ..        ..           ..       ..        ..
                    .         .            .        .           .




                                                                             4/10
                                    Real Numbers

 Proposition 1

    There is no rational number whose square equals 2.

                  √
In other words,       2 is not a rational number.




                                                         5/10
                                    Real Numbers

 Proposition 1

    There is no rational number whose square equals 2.

                  √
In other words,       2 is not a rational number.

Proof
                                                    p 2
  ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that
                                                      
                                                    q
                                                        = 2.

  ◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number.
    Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.




                                                                                 5/10
                                    Real Numbers

 Proposition 1

    There is no rational number whose square equals 2.

                  √
In other words,       2 is not a rational number.

Proof
                                                    p 2
  ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that
                                                      
                                                    q
                                                        = 2.

  ◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number.
    Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.

  ◦ Write the expression as p2 = 2q 2 — since p2 is even, so is p.

  ◦ Expressing p = 2k, we obtain p2 = 4k2 = 2q 2 .




                                                                                 5/10
                                    Real Numbers

 Proposition 1

    There is no rational number whose square equals 2.

                  √
In other words,       2 is not a rational number.

Proof
                                                    p 2
  ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that
                                                      
                                                    q
                                                        = 2.

  ◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number.
    Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.

  ◦ Write the expression as p2 = 2q 2 — since p2 is even, so is p.

  ◦ Expressing p = 2k, we obtain p2 = 4k2 = 2q 2 .

  ◦ Since q 2 = 2k2 is even, so is q.




                                                                                 5/10
                              Real Numbers



√
    2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R:
                      R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.




                                                                   6/10
                              Real Numbers



√
    2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R:
                      R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.

R together with the standard arithmetic operations is an ordered space:
for any x, y ∈ R, either x ≥ y or y ≥ x, where
                       x ≥ y ⇐⇒ (x − y) ≥ 0.




                                                                          6/10
                                 Real Numbers



√
    2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R:
                          R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.

R together with the standard arithmetic operations is an ordered space:
for any x, y ∈ R, either x ≥ y or y ≥ x, where
                          x ≥ y ⇐⇒ (x − y) ≥ 0.

The n-dimensional Euclidean space is defined by:
    Rn = R × . . . × R ≡ x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) : xi ∈ R, i = 1, 2, . . . , n
                          
           |   {z    }
                n times




                                                                                6/10
                                Euclidean Spaces


Write x ≥ y if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n.
  ◦ But note that Rn , unlike R, is not fully ordered.

  ◦ In R2 , x = (1, 0) and y = (0, 1) cannot be compared according to ≥.




                                                                           7/10
                                Euclidean Spaces


Write x ≥ y if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n.
  ◦ But note that Rn , unlike R, is not fully ordered.

  ◦ In R2 , x = (1, 0) and y = (0, 1) cannot be compared according to ≥.


The Euclidean space Rn is a vector space with three basic operations.
  ◦ Vector addition: x + y = (x1 + y1 , . . . , xn + yn ).

  ◦ Scalar multiplication: α z = (αz1 , . . . , αzn ).

  ◦ Inner (dot) product: x · y = x1 y1 + . . . + xn yn .

These operations are very important.

                                                     8/10
                        Vector Operations in Rn


The following holds for all x, y, z in Rn and all α, β in R:
  ◦ x + y = y + x;

  ◦ x + (y + z) = (x + y) + z;




                                                               9/10
                        Vector Operations in Rn


The following holds for all x, y, z in Rn and all α, β in R:
  ◦ x + y = y + x;

  ◦ x + (y + z) = (x + y) + z;

  ◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

  ◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;




                                                               9/10
                        Vector Operations in Rn


The following holds for all x, y, z in Rn and all α, β in R:
  ◦ x + y = y + x;

  ◦ x + (y + z) = (x + y) + z;

  ◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

  ◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;

  ◦ x · y = y · x;

  ◦ α(x · y) = (α x) · y = x · (α y);




                                                               9/10
                          Vector Operations in Rn


The following holds for all x, y, z in Rn and all α, β in R:
  ◦ x + y = y + x;

  ◦ x + (y + z) = (x + y) + z;

  ◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

  ◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;

  ◦ x · y = y · x;

  ◦ α(x · y) = (α x) · y = x · (α y);

  ◦ x · 0 = 0;

  ◦ x = x1 e1 + x2 e2 + . . . + xn en , where ei = (0, . . . , 0, 1, 0, . . . , 0).




                                                                                      9/10
                      Economic Applications

Example 1

 There are n goods in a competitive market.
   ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities.
   ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.




                                                                         10/10
                        Economic Applications

Example 1

 There are n goods in a competitive market.
   ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities.
   ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.

 The consumption space is X = Rn
                               +.

   ◦ A consumption bundle x ∈ X is a vector x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) such
     that xi ≥ 0 for all i = 1, . . . , n.




                                                                          10/10
                        Economic Applications

Example 1

 There are n goods in a competitive market.
   ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities.
   ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.

 The consumption space is X = Rn
                               +.

   ◦ A consumption bundle x ∈ X is a vector x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) such
     that xi ≥ 0 for all i = 1, . . . , n.

 Given prices p ≥ 0, the budget set for a consumer with wealth w is
                              
                 B(p, w) = x ∈ X : p · x ≤ w .




                                                                          10/10
